title: Airfoil
content: An airfoil is the cross-sectional shape of a wing, blade, or sail. It is designed to generate lift when moving through air. The shape determines how air flows around it and how much lift and drag are produced.

title: Lift
content: Lift is the upward force that opposes weight and keeps an aircraft in the air. It is generated by the pressure difference between the upper and lower surfaces of the airfoil. According to Bernoulli's principle, faster-moving air over the curved upper surface creates lower pressure, while slower air under the flatter lower surface creates higher pressure.
formula: L' = 0.5 × ρ × V² × c × CL (per meter span)

title: Drag
content: Drag is the aerodynamic force that opposes the forward motion of an aircraft. It is caused by air resistance, friction, and the creation of lift (induced drag). Minimizing drag is essential for efficient flight.
formula: D' = 0.5 × ρ × V² × c × CD (per meter span)

title: Angle of Attack (AoA)
content: The angle of attack is the angle between the chord line of the airfoil and the direction of the oncoming air (relative wind). Increasing AoA generally increases lift up to a point, but too high an AoA causes stall.

title: Stall
content: Stall occurs when the angle of attack exceeds a critical value, causing the airflow to separate from the upper surface of the airfoil. This results in a sudden loss of lift and increase in drag. Different airfoils have different stall angles.

title: Lift Coefficient (CL)
content: The lift coefficient is a dimensionless number that relates the lift generated by an airfoil to the fluid density, velocity, and chord length. It depends on the airfoil shape, angle of attack, and surface conditions.

title: Drag Coefficient (CD)
content: The drag coefficient is a dimensionless number that quantifies the drag resistance of an airfoil. It includes skin friction drag, pressure drag, and induced drag due to lift generation.

title: Lift-to-Drag Ratio (L/D)
content: The lift-to-drag ratio is a measure of aerodynamic efficiency. A higher L/D ratio means the airfoil produces more lift for each unit of drag. Gliders have very high L/D ratios, while fighter jets may have lower ratios.

title: Camber
content: Camber is the curvature of an airfoil. A cambered airfoil is curved on the upper surface (and sometimes the lower). Camber helps generate lift even at zero angle of attack by creating a pressure difference.

title: Chord Line
content: The chord line is a straight line connecting the leading edge to the trailing edge of the airfoil. The chord length is an important reference dimension for all aerodynamic calculations.

title: Pressure Difference
content: Lift is created by the pressure difference between the upper and lower surfaces of the airfoil. The upper surface has lower pressure (faster airflow), while the lower surface has higher pressure (slower airflow). This pressure difference results in a net upward force.

title: Flow Separation
content: Flow separation occurs when the boundary layer detaches from the surface of the airfoil. This happens at high angles of attack and leads to stall. Flow separation causes a large increase in drag and a decrease in lift.
